head: post-A4

findings.title: KERNEL-B-007 compensateMutation staging cleanup path untested
findings.invariant: KERNEL-B-007 — reject/compensate clean up staging file
findings.evidence: describe('KERNEL-B-007 — Buffer side-channel via contentHash') has one test 'reject cleanup' (line 211) but no test for 'compensate cleanup'. cluster-kernel.ts:917-923 explicitly calls this.deleteStagingFile() in compensateMutation as 'belt-and-suspenders cleanup'. The dispatch invariant states 'reject/compensate clean up staging' but only the reject half is probed.
findings.missing_assertion: expect(existsSync(stagingPath)).toBe(false) after compensateMutation on a committed ingest_artifact command. Pre-fix vs. post-fix divergence: a regression that removes the belt-and-suspenders deleteStagingFile call in compensateMutation would not be caught.
findings.recommendation: Add `it('compensate cleanup: a committed ingest_artifact whose staging file somehow survived gets swept on compensate')`: propose+validate+commit an artifact, then manually re-create the staging file via writeFileSync(stagingPath, content), then await kernel.compensateMutation(command.id, 'operator', 'reason'); expect(existsSync(stagingPath)).toBe(false).
findings.family_check: Section (a) both-halves: REJECT tested at line 211, COMPENSATE untested. Similar shape gap: KERNEL-B-007's commit-time tamper test (line 129) preserves the staging file on tamper, but no test verifies the staging file IS deleted on the success path when the tamper error path was previously hit and then a re-propose succeeds — i.e., 'tampered then retry' interaction.

findings.title: No orphan-sweep test for kernel pending-content staging directory
findings.invariant: KERNEL-B-007 — staging file lifecycle bounded; orphans don't accumulate
findings.evidence: ClusterKernel.getStagingDir() (cluster-kernel.ts:120-125) creates `.db-cluster/pending-content/` lazily on first call but the constructor never registers an orphan sweep for it. If process dies between propose's writeFileSync(tmpPath) and renameSync(tmpPath, stagingPath) (cluster-kernel.ts:520-521) the random-suffix `.<pid>-<rand>.tmp` file lingers forever. The adapters all have cleanupOrphanTmpFiles() at construction; the kernel's staging dir has no equivalent.
findings.missing_assertion: expect(existsSync(orphanStagingTmp)).toBe(false) after constructing a fresh ClusterKernel against a dataDir that contains a backdated orphan staging tmp file. Or, at minimum, the test family should include an interaction probe that exercises propose-then-crash-before-rename and proves nothing is reachable on the next start.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) add an assertion test that plants an orphan at `.db-cluster/pending-content/<hash>.<pid>-<rand>.tmp` with mtime >10min old, constructs a new ClusterKernel, and expects the orphan to be swept; OR (b) explicitly document the design choice that staging dir has no sweep and convert this finding to a HIGH severity LEAK rather than a missing-test finding.
findings.family_check: Section (e) cross-invariant: KERNEL-B-007 staging interacts with STORES-B-001 orphan-cleanup discipline. The adapters added sweeps; the kernel's parallel staging dir is the only `.tmp`-using directory in the codebase that has NO sweep. This is a missed family member.

findings.title: Kernel staging tmp suffix uses incompatible regex shape vs. sweep regex
findings.invariant: KERNEL-B-007 + STORES-B-001 — tmp file shape consistent for orphan sweep
findings.evidence: cluster-kernel.ts:519 uses `randomBytes(8).toString('hex')` → 16 hex chars. tmp-cleanup.ts:74 orphan regex is `\.\d+-[a-z0-9]{1,6}\.tmp$` — 1-6 chars. Even if a sweep WERE wired for the staging dir, the kernel's tmp files would never match. Tests do not probe this shape consistency.
findings.missing_assertion: Property: `buildRandomTmpPath(p)` matches the orphan-sweep regex for any p. Also: kernel's staging tmp shape matches the same regex. Neither is asserted today.
findings.recommendation: Add a structural test: assert `/\.\d+-[a-z0-9]{1,6}\.tmp$/.test(buildRandomTmpPath('foo'))` is true, AND that the kernel's staging tmp shape (currently hex(8)) also matches OR document the design choice. Best fix: kernel switches to buildRandomTmpPath() so both producer and consumer share the same shape.
findings.family_check: Section (c) regression a future change wouldn't catch: if someone changes Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 8) to slice(2, 12) — perfectly reasonable — the orphan sweep regex no longer matches and orphan accumulation resumes silently. NO test would fail.

findings.title: CommandQueue.persist still uses fixed `.tmp` suffix — uncovered by STORES-B-001 sentinel
findings.invariant: STORES-B-001 — no `${path}.tmp` fixed-suffix race remains in atomic-write codebases
findings.evidence: command-queue.ts:117 still has `const tmpPath = `${this.filePath}.tmp`;` — the exact regression pattern the Wave A4 dispatch tagged as the STORES-B-001 fix shape. The STORES-B-001 sentinel tests (lines 87-179) only probe the four LocalXStore.persist methods + LocalArtifactStore.ingest/importSnapshot content writes. CommandQueue lives under src/kernel/ not src/adapters/local/, but it has the SAME race condition.
findings.missing_assertion: Source-level assert: `command-queue.ts` persist() body either uses `buildRandomTmpPath(this.filePath)` OR has no `${path}.tmp` fixed-suffix string. None of the current sentinels probe this file.
findings.recommendation: Extend the STORES-B-001 source-level regression sentinel to ALSO scan src/kernel/command-queue.ts. Either fix the file (preferred — same multi-process race exists) and add the sentinel, or document the design choice (e.g., CommandQueue is single-process-only) and exclude it explicitly so future audits don't re-discover this gap.
findings.family_check: Section (g) family-of-call-sites: the STORES-B-001 finding was 'all four local adapters write to `${path}.tmp`' — but the SAME bug shape exists in CommandQueue.persist. The audit + fix scoped to /adapters/local/ missed the sibling file in /kernel/.

findings.title: SURFACE-B-001 sibling site — cluster_resolve index-URI arm not probed
findings.invariant: SURFACE-B-001 — every IndexRecord returned across MCP surfaces is metadata-stripped
findings.evidence: Only cluster_find_sources is tested (lines 76-130). server.ts:570-571 also wraps `cluster_resolve` for `resolved.store === 'index'` through sanitizeIndexRecordForOutput — but no Wave A4 test exercises that path. A future regression that removes the `else if (resolved.store === 'index')` arm would not fail Wave A4 nets. Wave A3 (AGG-001) added this arm — Wave A4 should sentinel it.
findings.missing_assertion: expect(record.metadata).toBeUndefined() and expect(record._sourceType).toBe('derivative') when handleTool('cluster_resolve', { uri: 'cluster://index/<id>' }) is called on a seeded index record.
findings.recommendation: Add `it('cluster_resolve on index:// URI strips metadata and attaches _sourceType=derivative')`: seed cluster, list index records via SDK, call handleTool('cluster_resolve', { uri: `cluster://index/${indexRecord.id}` }) and assert result.object.metadata is undefined and result.object._sourceType==='derivative'.
findings.family_check: Section (g) family-of-call-sites probe: dispatch explicitly asks 'is there a similar untested LIST arm in cluster_retrieve_bundle.indexRecords?' Answer: cluster_retrieve_bundle does NOT return indexRecords (server.ts:496-531 omits them). BUT cluster_resolve DOES return an index object for index:// URIs and is not covered by Wave A4 tests.

findings.title: Property test does not assert staging-file deletion or orphan absence post-commit
findings.invariant: KERNEL-B-007 — staging file deleted on success across the entire input domain
findings.evidence: Property test (lines 242-286) asserts `ingested!.equals(content)` but does NOT assert (a) the staging file at pending-content/<hash> is gone post-commit, (b) no orphan `.<pid>-<rand>.tmp` file remains in pending-content/, (c) no orphan tmp file remains in the artifact content dir. The 'commit happy path' single-example test (line 166) does assert (a) but the property test covers 50 random inputs without it.
findings.missing_assertion: Inside the asyncProperty after the .equals(content) check: `expect(existsSync(join(dir, 'pending-content', contentHash))).toBe(false);` AND `const stagingDirContents = existsSync(join(dir, 'pending-content')) ? readdirSync(join(dir, 'pending-content')) : []; expect(stagingDirContents).toEqual([]);`
findings.recommendation: Add the two assertions above inside the property body. Same for orphan check in the artifact content dir: `readdirSync(join(dir, 'content')).filter(n=>n.endsWith('.tmp'))` must be empty.
findings.family_check: Section (d) property-based vs example-based: the property test currently probes only the input domain for content roundtrip equality. The staging-file lifecycle invariant is a separate axis the property could also cover with negligible additional cost.

findings.title: STORES-B-003 idempotency tests don't probe nested-object key-order variance
findings.evidence: Idempotency tests (lines 348-373, 404-423, 449-468, 498-519) call importX with `{ ...event }` — a shallow-spread copy that preserves source insertion key order. Real-world re-imports go through JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(record)) which can reorder nested object keys (the V8 implementation preserves insertion order, but the spec does not guarantee this for property strings that look like array indices). assertContentMatch uses JSON.stringify(({...x, owner: undefined})), so the comparison is sensitive to key-order in nested objects (e.g., `detail`, `attributes`, `metadata`).
findings.missing_assertion: An idempotency test where the second import passes the record through JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(record)) before passing to importX, simulating a realistic round-trip. Should still return existing (not throw).
findings.recommendation: Add `it('importEvent idempotent across JSON round-trip')`: const rt = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(event)); const second = await store.importEvent(rt); expect(second.id).toBe(event.id) (no throw). Same for importReceipt / canonical.importSnapshot / artifact.importSnapshot.
findings.family_check: Section (c) regression caught: a future refactor could swap `JSON.stringify` for `canonicalize` (sort keys) or vice versa, and the change in semantics would silently flip idempotent → throwing on JSON-roundtripped restore inputs. No current test would catch this.

findings.title: SURFACE-B-002 'loaded policy used' test asserts marker substring only, not decision direction
findings.invariant: SURFACE-B-002 — with policies.json present, decisions reflect loaded policies
findings.evidence: Lines 135-186: test asserts `out.toContain('WAVE-A4-LOADED-POLICY-DENY-MARKER')`. This passes as long as the loaded policy was matched AND its reason text is echoed somewhere in stdout. It does NOT assert (a) Decision: DENY appeared, (b) Matched policy: wave-a4-custom-deny appeared, (c) the deny was NOT due to a default-policy fallthrough that happens to contain the marker substring by coincidence (defense-in-depth).
findings.missing_assertion: expect(out).toMatch(/Decision: DENY/) AND expect(out).toContain('wave-a4-custom-deny') (the policy id, not just the reason marker).
findings.recommendation: Tighten the two tests at lines 135-162 and 164-186 to assert THREE things: (1) the loaded policy id appears, (2) the decision direction is DENY, (3) the marker reason string appears. Today only (3) is asserted.
findings.family_check: Section (a) both-halves: the test asserts the LOADED policy's REASON appears but doesn't check the DECISION matches the loaded policy's decision field. A regression that uses the loaded policy's reason text but the wrong decision would slip through.

findings.title: SURFACE-B-003 redactError does not test cluster_find_sources/cluster_resolve etc. error paths
findings.invariant: SURFACE-B-003 — every MCP CallToolRequest error path uses redactError before returning
findings.evidence: Tests probe redactError() directly (lines 231-309) and one synthetic 'cluster_nonexistent_tool' invocation (line 310). The actual MCP catch arm in server.ts:823-837 is the integration point — the test exercises the FUNCTION but not the WIRING. A future change that removes the `catch (err)` arm in server.ts, or routes a tool to return the raw error (e.g., `throw err` instead of catching), or accidentally surfaces stack traces, would not fail any Wave A4 test.
findings.missing_assertion: Integration test: invoke a real MCP tool path that internally throws (e.g., cluster_resolve with malformed URI, or cluster_commit_mutation with non-existent commandId) and parse the actual CallToolRequest response shape (`{content: [{type:'text', text: '...'}], isError: true}`); assert the parsed JSON has `code` and `error` fields and that no absolute paths leak.
findings.recommendation: Mock the MCP server's CallToolRequest handler invocation OR use spawn the actual MCP process via stdio and send a real tool call that triggers an internal throw, then assert the response shape contains code + sanitized message.
findings.family_check: Section (b) full invariant vs surface: SURFACE-B-003 has TWO halves — (i) redactError logic correct, (ii) MCP catch arm USES redactError. Half (i) is well tested. Half (ii) is unit-only via direct function call; the actual wiring through the MCP server's catch is not integration-tested.

findings.title: redactError test for typed ClusterError messages does not assert path-scrubbing in the message
findings.invariant: SURFACE-B-003 — absolute paths scrubbed even when error is a typed ClusterError subclass
findings.evidence: Test at line 248 uses NotFoundError('canonical', 'entity-xyz') — its message does not contain any path. Test only asserts `sanitized.code === 'NOT_FOUND'`. The OTHER half — that a typed ClusterError's path-containing message ALSO gets scrubbed — is never tested. sanitize.ts:174-179 does call scrubMessage(err.message) for ClusterError, but a regression that removes the scrubMessage call on the typed-error branch would not fail any test.
findings.missing_assertion: A test that constructs a ClusterError subclass whose message includes an absolute path, then expects sanitized.message NOT to contain the path: e.g., `class TestClusterErr extends ClusterError { constructor() { super('failed reading /etc/passwd', 'TEST_ERR'); }}; expect(redactError(new TestClusterErr()).message).not.toContain('/etc/passwd');`
findings.recommendation: Add the test described above. The typed-error path and the built-in-error path both need explicit path-scrubbing coverage.
findings.family_check: Section (a) both-halves: typed-error CODE mapping is tested (line 248); typed-error PATH SCRUBBING is not. Built-in error path scrubbing IS tested (lines 231-247) for both Posix and Windows paths.

findings.title: STORES-B-001 mass-migration source-level test uses indexOf('private persist(') which silently passes if the method is renamed
findings.invariant: STORES-B-001 — sentinel for source-level regression of fixed `.tmp` suffix
findings.evidence: Lines 99, 132, 144, 157, 165, 172: `const persistStart = src.indexOf('private persist(');` followed by `expect(persistStart).toBeGreaterThan(-1)`. If a future refactor renames `private persist()` to `protected persist()` or `private write()` or extracts it into a helper, indexOf returns -1 and the expect fails — that's correct. But if a refactor changes the method to a different shape (e.g., consolidates two methods into one) the test merely fails-to-find rather than verifying the new shape uses random tmp. Brittle to renames but blind to consolidation.
findings.missing_assertion: Assert the COUNT of `buildRandomTmpPath` calls in each source file matches an expected minimum (e.g., LocalArtifactStore.ts must call buildRandomTmpPath >=3 times: persist, ingest content, importSnapshot content). Today the tests only check that the method body contains the pattern; a refactor that inlines or moves these calls could silently lose one.
findings.recommendation: Convert each source-level test to count occurrences: `const helperCalls = (src.match(/buildRandomTmpPath\s*\(/g) || []).length; expect(helperCalls).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(EXPECTED_FOR_FILE);`
findings.family_check: Section (c) regression a refactor wouldn't catch: future refactor that consolidates persist() into a shared base class would orphan the indexOf('private persist(') sentinel; the test would fail loudly (correct), but if the base class itself has no test, the random-tmp invariant could regress in the base class with NO test catching it.

findings.title: STORES-B-015 cycle detection tests don't probe MULTI-hop cycles (A→B→C→A)
findings.invariant: STORES-B-015 — visited-Set guard prevents any-length cycle
findings.evidence: Tests cover (i) 2-hop A↔B cycle (line 555), (ii) self-cycle A→A (line 593), (iii) linear A→B→C no-false-positive (line 615). Missing: a multi-hop cycle A→B→C→A (3 hops) or a long-tail cycle (e.g., A→B→C→D→B — partial linear tail then loop). The visited-Set implementation handles any length, but a regression that uses a 2-element history buffer instead of a Set would catch the 2-hop and self-cycle cases but NOT a 3-hop cycle.
findings.missing_assertion: Append a/b/c, poison a.parentEventId = c.id (forming b→a→c→b cycle); expect trace(b.id) to throw LedgerCycleDetectedError.
findings.recommendation: Add a 3-hop cycle test as described. Optionally also a 5-hop test for additional confidence.
findings.family_check: Section (c) regression risk: a future 'optimization' that replaces visited Set with a fixed-size LRU (motivated by 'most cycles are short') would pass the existing tests and silently break long-cycle detection.

findings.title: TESTS-B-002 cleanup-pattern sentinel passes even if afterEach body is a no-op
findings.evidence: Line 165: `expect(source).toMatch(/afterEach\s*\(\s*\(\s*\)\s*=>\s*{/);` and line 166: `expect(source).toMatch(/rmSync\s*\(\s*dir\s*,\s*\{\s*recursive:\s*true/);`. Both regexes match anywhere in the file. A future refactor that has an `afterEach(() => { /* TODO: implement cleanup */ })` PLUS a separate `rmSync(dir, {recursive: true})` in a totally unrelated test block would pass both regexes while leaking dirs.
findings.missing_assertion: Behavioural assertion: stat or readdir the tmpdir() before and after running wave6-policy-proof's afterEach, prove the tracked-dir count goes to zero. Or, at minimum, assert the afterEach body itself contains a call to rmSync (not just that both patterns exist somewhere in the file).
findings.recommendation: Replace the two separate regex matches with a single multiline match that anchors the rmSync call inside the afterEach body. E.g., `expect(source).toMatch(/afterEach[\s\S]*?_trackedDirs\.pop[\s\S]*?rmSync/);`
findings.family_check: Section (c) regression risk: the test as-written confirms the FILE has the words; not that the SEMANTICS of cleanup are in place. A refactor that breaks the pop()/loop wiring could escape detection.

findings.title: TESTS-B-001 sentinel restricts pattern to `.test-` literal but newer test patterns could elude it
findings.invariant: TESTS-B-001/B-007 — no in-repo TEST_DIR pattern remains
findings.evidence: Regex `/join\s*\(\s*import\.meta\.dirname\s*,\s*['"]\.test-/` only matches literal `.test-` prefix. A future test that uses `join(import.meta.dirname, '.scratch-XYZ')` or `join(import.meta.dirname, 'tmp-')` or `path.resolve(import.meta.dirname, 'work')` would re-introduce the in-repo working-dir flake without triggering this sentinel.
findings.missing_assertion: Broader sentinel: ANY use of `import.meta.dirname` as the root for a writable directory should fail unless explicitly allowlisted. Or, assert positively: every test file that writes to disk uses tmpdir().
findings.recommendation: Either (a) broaden the regex to `/join\s*\(\s*import\.meta\.dirname\s*,\s*['"][^'"]*['"]\s*\)/` and require the resulting path is NOT used as mkdirSync/writeFileSync target, OR (b) accept the LOW severity — the literal `.test-` pattern was the actual offender; future patterns are speculative.
findings.family_check: Section (b) full invariant vs surface: the FULL invariant is 'no in-repo writable working dirs in tests'. The sentinel probes only the SPECIFIC FAILED PATTERN. A discipline shift to a different prefix would escape detection.

findings.title: TESTS-B-004 typed-tuple sentinel checks for ONE destructure shape; alternative valid shapes would fail spuriously
findings.invariant: TESTS-B-004 — makePolicyKernel returns {restricted, admin}; no __admin cast
findings.evidence: Line 219: `expect(source).toMatch(/const\s*\{\s*restricted\s*:\s*pk\s*,\s*admin\s*:\s*adminK\s*\}\s*=\s*makePolicyKernel/);` hard-codes the variable names `pk` and `adminK`. A perfectly valid future refactor that destructures as `const { restricted, admin } = makePolicyKernel(...)` (no rename) OR `const { restricted: kernel, admin: adminKernel } = ...` (different rename) would fail this sentinel even though the typed-tuple invariant is preserved.
findings.missing_assertion: Looser sentinel: `expect(source).toMatch(/makePolicyKernel.*=>\s*\{[\s\S]*restricted[\s\S]*admin[\s\S]*\}/m)` — check the return statement / interface, not the call-site destructure shape.
findings.recommendation: Loosen the call-site destructure check OR add a separate check for the interface declaration only and remove the call-site-shape check.
findings.family_check: Section (c) regression a refactor wouldn't catch (inverted): the current test would FAIL on a legitimate refactor while STILL passing if someone re-added a `__admin` cast in a different style (e.g., `(restricted as any).__admin =`). Test is brittle-and-incomplete simultaneously.

summary: Audited 4 Wave A4 regression test files (32 test cases across 11 invariants). Found 15 evidence-bearing gaps where the test as written does not probe the FULL invariant. The most consequential finding family is the kernel `pending-content/` staging directory: KERNEL-B-007 introduced this storage area but (a) has no orphan sweep wired at construction (V3-A4-002), (b) uses a tmp suffix shape incompatible with the existing adapter sweep regex (V3-A4-003), and (c) lacks a compensate-path cleanup test (V3-A4-001). Combined with the discovery that CommandQueue.persist STILL uses the fixed `.tmp` suffix the STORES-B-001 fix was supposed to retire (V3-A4-004), there is a coherent gap shape: the Wave A4 fixes addressed the surfaces explicitly named in the audit but missed sibling files with identical bug shapes. The property test for KERNEL-B-007 also under-asserts — it confirms content roundtrip but not staging-file lifecycle (V3-A4-006). Source-level sentinels for STORES-B-001 and TESTS-B-* are too narrow (V3-A4-011, V3-A4-013, V3-A4-014, V3-A4-015) — they catch the LITERAL pattern that flagged the audit but miss adjacent regressions.
lens_quality_self_assessment: High confidence on V3-A4-001 through V3-A4-005 (CRITICAL/HIGH) — each names a specific file:line, the missing assertion, and a recommendation grounded in code I read. V3-A4-002, V3-A4-003, and V3-A4-004 in particular surface real bug-class siblings that the Wave A4 fixes did not cover. Medium confidence on V3-A4-006 through V3-A4-010 — these are test-completeness gaps where the underlying invariant is correctly implemented but a specific input/path is not probed; severity is MEDIUM because a regression would need to break the specific path while preserving the tested paths. Low confidence on V3-A4-012 through V3-A4-015 — these are sentinel-brittleness findings where the current sentinel is technically sufficient for the immediate regression class but would not survive a refactor; severity LOW because they require both a future refactor AND a new bug to slip through. Did NOT audit: CIDOCS slice widening (no test in Wave A4) and TESTS-B-001 wave6-proof empirical determinism (also no Wave A4 sentinel, but the migration itself is the proof). Stayed under 15 findings cap; all are evidence-bearing.
